---
title: "About"
description: "Professional profile of Miguel Cosenza Contreras."
---

Miguel Cosenza Contreras is a proteomics bioinformatician and data scientist based in Freiburg im Breisgau, Germany. He works at the interface between experimental and clinical questions and computational analysis of high-throughput mass-spectrometry-based proteomics data.

Miguel trained as a biologist and developed a research profile focused on applied proteomics bioinformatics, clinical proteomics, and reproducible data analysis. His work spans proteomics, terminomics, lipidomics, proteogenomics, and clinical multi-omics integration, with applications in cancer biology, cardiac disease, host-parasite interactions, protease biology, and protein interaction research.

Since 2024, Miguel has worked as a postdoctoral researcher / bioinformatician in the Laboratory of Protease Degradomics and SFB1381 at the Faculty of Biology, University of Freiburg, with Prof. Dr. Pitter Huesgen. His current work focuses on proteolytic processing, terminomics, protease degradomics, and protein interaction research in the context of SFB1381.

From 2019 to 2024, he was a doctoral researcher in the Laboratory of Translational Proteomics at the Institute of Surgical Pathology, University Medical Center Freiburg, supervised by Prof. Dr. Oliver Schilling. His doctoral work focused on proteomics data science and multi-omics data integration for exploratory insight into disease mechanisms.

A recurring theme across his work is reproducibility: using R, Quarto, transparent project structure, and reusable analysis code to make complex proteomics workflows easier to inspect, adapt, and extend.

## Research Themes

::: {.content-grid}
::: {.info-card}
### Clinical Proteomics
Quantitative proteomics and biomarker-oriented analysis in translational disease studies.
:::

::: {.info-card}
### Proteolytic Processing
Computational analysis of protein termini, cleavage events, and protease activity.
:::

::: {.info-card}
### Multi-omics Data Science
Integration of proteomics with lipidomics, proteogenomics, and clinical variables.
:::

::: {.info-card}
### Reproducible Analysis
Human-readable R and Quarto workflows for mass-spectrometry data analysis.
:::
:::
